data.title: Why I Just Loaded Up On Aris Mining Ahead Of 2026
data.description:
With the recent flurry of news, it’s a good time to reassess the risk-reward profile of Aris Mining (ARMN). In this update, I’ll start by comparing Aris with 22 gold-mining peers to identify the key drivers behind their sustained market outperformance or underperformance—in other words, why some become industry leaders while others fall behind. I’ll then circle back and evaluate Aris in light of those drivers, with the goal of gaining a deeper understanding of this emerging gold producer in Latin America.

Here’s how I separate the winning gold stocks from the dogs

The group

I selected 22 gold producers as Aris Mining’s comparison group, including three Australia-focused miners and four companies that operate exclusively in Africa. I had to limit the number of companies in the study, which unfortunately means leaving out some solid names. However, this selection bias shouldn’t prevent the key trends from emerging.

  • The Australian sub-group is: Gold Road Resources (ELKMF), Northern Star Resources (NESRF), and Westgold (WGXRF).
  • The African sub-group is: Caledonia Mining (CMCL), Pan African Resources (PAFRF), Perseus Mining (PMNXF), and Resolute Mining (RMGGF).
  • The remainder of the comparison group includes: Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM), Alamos Gold (AGI), Barrick Gold (B), Centerra Gold (CGAU), Eldorado Gold (EGO), Equinox Gold (EQX), G Mining Ventures (GMINF), Hochschild Mining (HCHDF), IAMGOLD (IAG), Kinross Gold (KGC), Lundin Gold (LUGDF), Newmont (NEM), SSR Mining (SSRM).

I decided to use EV per in-situ gold ounce as the primary valuation metric. Statistically, about 69.3% of a gold producer’s enterprise value can be explained by its in-situ gold-equivalent resources, as indicated by the R² of 0.693. That suggests a strong relationship, leaving 30.7% unexplained, which can be attributable to other factors such as resource quality (average grade, orebody continuity, open-pittability, metallurgy), cost structure (including infrastructure access) and margins, development stage, and above-ground risks (fiscal terms, currency, permitting, jurisdiction), as illustrated in Figure 1. It is worth noting that the EV/Production metric actually has a weak positive linear relationship with EV, as evidenced by an R² of 0.168.

Ounces Are Not Equal: The hidden variables behind headline in-situ resource numbers

Like any resource investment, gold miners compete on costs, typically measured by all-in sustaining cost (AISC). AISC represents the total cost to sustain current mining operations, including direct production costs, sustaining capital expenditures, exploration expenses, and corporate G&A. AISC tends to rise naturally as grades decline and mining costs increase over time—especially under less-frugal operators.

There are countless ways to end up with a high AISC. However, there are really only two reliable ways to drive it down: either by mining an exceptionally continuous, high-grade orebody, as at Lundin Gold’s Fruta del Norte mine, or by mining open pits followed by heap-leach operations, as illustrated in Figure 2. The former is straightforward: when your average grade is five times the industry average, you only need to mine and process one-fifth of the ore to produce the same ounces of gold—and have one-fifth of the tailings to store. The latter—exemplified by G Mining (Tocantinzinho, Oko West), B2Gold (Goose, Back River, Gramalote, Fekola, Otjikoto, Masbate), and Perseus (Edikan, Sissingué, Yaouré, Meyas Sand, Nyanzaga)—deserves more investor attention. It turns out you can still achieve industry-leading costs even when your mines have average grades, as long as you mine at surface and process oxide ore through heap leaching at exceptionally low cost. Regarding cost, especially energy intensity, the variance between open-pit and underground mining is debated in the literature. Some studies find open-pit mining to be significantly less costly, while others suggest the opposite. These differing views may stem from sampling differences. Ultimately, cost depends on the specifics of each mine—for example, the strip ratio for open pits and the orebody depth and grades for underground mines. That said, our data indicate that the lowest AISC values are found both in open-pit operations and in extremely high-grade underground mines, such as Fruta del Norte.

By the way, I touched on this strategy in a previous article. Minera Alamos (MAIFF) attempted to follow the open-pit, heap-leach model in Mexico. Although its progress was slowed by permitting under the Sheinbaum administration, the strategy itself remains valid.

The standouts: The Hidden Drivers of Gold Stock Valuations

Of these 23 gold miners, three clearly stand out as especially favored by the market: Agnico Eagle, Alamos Gold, and Lundin Gold, as shown in figures 3 and 4.

Lundin Gold represents a miner’s dream. Its Fruta del Norte mine in Ecuador hosts one of the highest concentrations of gold within a compact volume measuring 1.3 km long, 60–160 m wide, and 300 m subvertical, as evidenced in Figure 5. FDN boasts high grades (averaging 6.6 g/t Au—five times the industry average, as shown in Figure 2), low costs (AISC of $1,036/oz Au), high margins (EBITDA margin >70% as of Q3 2025), and low subsurface risk (exceptional orebody continuity) as well as low above-ground risk (the host province of Zamora-Chinchipe is mining-friendly).

Alamos Gold is favored by the market for its track record of rapid production growth while lowering risk by focusing on safe jurisdictions—primarily Canada, where 89% of its NAV is located—and divesting assets in high-risk regions. Over the longer term, it expects to grow production to 900 kozpa from the 2025 mid-point guidance of 570 koz, all self-funded. By 2027, it believes economies of scale will drive its AISC down to $1,175/oz from the 2025 guidance of $1,425/oz, further expanding margins.

Agnico Eagle also focuses on safe jurisdictions—Canada, Australia, Mexico, and Finland—but it has achieved another level of diversified scale that provides highly visible long-term growth. Agnico is known for flawless execution of its growth plans and relentless cost control, almost in the style of Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ).

Under the previously discussed investment principle, companies like Agnico, Alamos Gold, and Lundin Gold are rewarded with premium valuation multiples because they can be counted on to reduce costs, maintain or even expand margins, and deliver profitable growth. These factors come together to drive value creation, which can be expressed as follows:

  • EV = [EV/EBITDA] × (Gold price − AISC) × Production × (1 + CAGR)ᵀⁱᵐᵉ
  • EV = [EV/In-situ resources] × In-situ resources = [EV/In-situ resources] × Resource life × Production × (1 + CAGR)ᵀⁱᵐᵉ

where the average resource life is around 50 years, as shown in Figure 6. Up to a certain point (more on this later), scaling up production reduces costs through economies of scale. Lower costs mean higher margins and greater resilience through the cycle.

These three market darlings share several other common traits. They all have squeaky-clean balance sheets: as of the end of Q3 2025, Lundin Gold had no debt and $494 million in cash; Alamos Gold had $213 million in net cash; and Agnico Eagle had $2.16 billion in net cash. Thanks to its aggressive debt reduction, Moody’s upgraded Agnico Eagle’s credit rating to A3 from Baa1. They also use their free cash flow to reward shareholders. Lundin Gold has been raising its annualized dividend at a 58.9% CAGR since initiating payouts in 2022; it currently offers a 4.2% yield. Alamos has paid dividends since 2010 and is now considering buybacks with $600 million of cash on its balance sheet. Agnico Eagle has paid dividends every year since 1983, currently yielding 1.0%, and has increased its quarterly dividend fivefold since the last gold bear-market bottom in 2015. In the first nine months of 2025, it repurchased 2.33 million common shares for $300 million.

How to pull away from the pack in 2025 and beyond

Size plays an important role in helping gold miners benefit from economies of scale, as we discussed above; however, bigger is not always better. Once a company reaches a certain level of in-situ gold-equivalent ounces—somewhere around 150–200 Moz AuEq, as shown in Figure 7—the market seems to stop rewarding the accumulation of additional underground resources. That is logical from several angles. Financially, holding millions of ounces you lack the capital or manpower to develop is a poor use of exploration or acquisition dollars. It is usually far more efficient to divest those non-core ounces to someone who can put them into production, and to use part of the proceeds to reduce interest-bearing debt—essentially following the path Alamos Gold has taken. Geologically, a company the size of Newmont can only chase very large deposits, but few giant primary gold deposits remain for a pure-play gold miner to grow. The great gold giants historically came from a limited set of deposit styles—mesothermal, Carlin-type, porphyry/epithermal, intrusion-related, Witwatersrand, VMS, and IOCG—but many of these are now largely exhausted and major new discoveries are rare. Porphyries, such as Filo del Sol, add another issue: own too many, and you drift toward being a copper producer, forfeiting the valuation premium of gold-focused miners. Lastly, from an organizational standpoint, taking on too many projects leads to corporate bloat—large headcounts, slow decision-making, and weak capital allocation.

I believe that over the next few years, as gold continues to move higher, we may see the emergence of a group of large-cap gold producers valued between $30 billion and $70 billion, as shown in Figure 8. This could happen through consolidation among the numerous junior and intermediate producers, or through a “whale-fall” effect—divestments of non-core mines or undeveloped resources that Newmont and Barrick have neither the capacity nor the intent to advance. It is an exciting time for growth-oriented companies like Aris Mining.

Now that we’ve pinpointed the key drivers of value creation in gold mining, let’s apply them to a deep look at Aris Mining. We’ll focus on four things: (1) whether Aris has a visible, achievable production-growth path as gold continues to move higher; (2) how the company intends to drive costs lower; (3) whether management is allocating capital and handling corporate finance prudently—especially when it comes to reducing debt; and (4) what concrete steps they’re taking to reward shareholders.

Production growth

Asset portfolio

In February 2020, Gran Colombia Gold Corp. spun off its Marmato Mine into Caldas Gold Corp., retaining a 71.8% ownership stake in Caldas Gold, ostensibly to accelerate the development of Marmato Lower (Figure 9). However, under then-CEO Serafino Iacono, Caldas went on to acquire South American Resources for its Juby project in Ontario.

By 2020, Canadian Business Hall of Famer Ian Telfer had overseen the $10 billion merger of Goldcorp with Newmont and retired as its chairman, while Neil Woodyer had just sold Leagold to Equinox Gold for $578 million. For readers unfamiliar with influential figures like Telfer and Woodyer in the Canadian mining sector, Telfer co-founded TVX Gold Inc. in 1983, growing it into a global mid-tier gold producer before merging it into Kinross Gold (KGC) in 1998. Together with Frank Giustra, he acquired Wheaton River Minerals Ltd. and, through a series of acquisitions, transformed it from a C$17 million shell into a company valued at $2.4 billion. In 2004 and 2005, they spun out Wheaton River’s silver-streaming business into Silver Wheaton Corp., which later became Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM), and merged Wheaton River with Goldcorp, where Telfer served as CEO (2005–2006) and Chairman (2006–2019). Woodyer founded Endeavour Financial Corp. in 1988 and beginning in 2009, together with Frank Giustra, transitioned it into Endeavour Mining (EDVMF). Through a series of acquisitions and new builds in West Africa, they developed it into a 500,000-oz-per-year gold producer operating four mines. In 2016, Woodyer stepped down as CEO of Endeavour Mining and founded the Mexico- and Brazil-focused Leagold Mining Corp., which was merged into Equinox Gold Corp. (EQX) four years later.

In November 2020, Telfer assembled an all-star team with Woodyer as CEO to take over Caldas and rename it Aris Gold, while concurrently raising $85 million, including $38 million from the incoming team, to advance the Marmato expansion project. In March 2022, Aris secured operatorship of the Soto Norte project in Colombia as the first step in building a portfolio of projects to support aggressive organic growth, as illustrated in Figure 10.

While the Telfer–Woodyer team laid the foundation for Aris Gold’s next decade, Gran Colombia Gold—later known as GCM Mining—went on a shopping spree, expanding hastily outside Colombia into Spain, Canada, and Guyana in search of asset diversification, and in the process squandering cash flows from the high-grade Segovia Mine. In the end, what it really needed was a serially successful leadership team like Telfer–Woodyer. Thus, in September 2022, GCM and Aris Gold recombined to form Aris Mining.

As a result of the GCM–Aris merger, Aris Mining inherited a portfolio of four core assets, as shown in Figure 11: the high-grade underground Segovia Mine; the underground Marmato Upper Mine with a bulk-mineable root; the high-grade Soto Norte deposit for underground mining; and the open-pittable Toroparu deposit in the Cuyuní–Mazaruni greenstone belt of the Guiana Shield, which forms part of the same contiguous geological province as the West African Shield (before the two were separated by the opening of the Central Atlantic)—a geology the Aris team knows extremely well from their Endeavour years. Together, these four assets give Aris a total of 32.05 Moz of measured, indicated, and inferred contained gold resources to support its organic growth objectives (Fig. 9).

Production growth outlook

At Segovia, Aris Mining completed and commissioned a second mill in June 2025, increasing processing capacity by 50% to 3,000 tpd. The company is currently ramping up operations toward 300,000 ozpa. At Marmato Lower, first gold is expected in H2 2026, and once ramp-up is complete, the mine is expected to produce 200,000 ozpa. I expect Aris to reach its 500,000 ozpa run-rate target by 2027.

Aris Mining plans to submit the formal environmental license application for Soto Norte to the Colombian environmental authority (ANLA) in H1 2026, which will initiate the regulatory review process. In Colombia, this typically includes technical evaluation, public participation, clarifications, and potential requests for additional information. Assuming the process takes roughly 24 months, as is often the case, permits may be obtained by mid-2028, with Soto Norte potentially beginning production by 2030 and adding 263,000 ozpa. That means Toroparu may pour first gold ahead of Soto Norte.

The Toroparu prefeasibility study is expected by October 2026. Aris Mining may reach FID by next November, although the company has already begun exploring ways to accelerate construction. Financing Toroparu will not be an issue, as the company is capable of self-funding the US$800 million build. I expect first gold in 2029, adding 235,000 ozpa upon ramp-up. This would put the company at roughly 1 Mozpa by then, implying a 21% CAGR in gold production over the next seven years, as illustrated in Figure 12.

Cost control

A gold miner’s AISC is determined by mining, processing, overhead, sustaining capex, exploration, and accrued reclamation costs. However, the gold price has a major impact: royalties (about 4% of revenue), local taxes (0.4–1%), community agreements, Contract Mining Partners (or CMP) agreements, and contractor rates all rise when gold moves higher in Colombia. In general, every $1,000/oz increase in the gold price can add roughly $120–$250/oz to AISC. In addition, the Colombian peso has weakened about 38% against the U.S. dollar since 2019, which has inflated USD-denominated costs such as equipment and chemicals, partly offset by local labor costs.

The roughly 85% increase in the gold price over the past five years would have pushed the Segovia Operations’ AISC to about $1,740/oz, based on historical sensitivity (Figure 13). In reality, Aris Mining kept AISC near $1,560/oz over the past two years, allowing only about a $460/oz rise from the 2019–2022 levels (Figure 14).

I actually believe Aris is well positioned to reduce Segovia’s AISC in the near future. CMP ore typically has lower head grade than ore from Aris’ own operations, which is partly why CMP-related AISC tends to be higher (Figure 15a & b). As recently as Q2 2024, CMPs accounted for about 53% of gold-sales volume, so their higher AISC has been the main reason Segovia’s overall AISC sits where it does. Since then, Aris-owned operations have steadily increased their share of total gold sold, setting the stage for Segovia’s AISC to converge toward the AISC reported from Aris’ own mining (Figure 15c).

In addition, with the second mill successfully commissioned in June 2025—boosting total capacity by 50% to 3,000 tpd (Figure 14)—Segovia has delivered a meaningful increase in throughput and production. Segovia milled an average of 2,553 tpd in Q3 2025, up 29.2% sequentially, and still 17.5% below nameplate capacity, while maintaining head grades near 10 g/t Au and recoveries around 96%. Gold production at Segovia rose 12.5% from Q2 to Q3 2025 (Figure 15d).

At the Marmato gold mine, Aris Mining is currently producing only from the upper, narrow-vein zone, using a mill designed for 1,250 tpd but realistically operating at a capacity of 1,000 tpd (Figure 16). Production from Marmato Lower—the bulk-mining zone—won’t begin until H2 2026, when the new 5,000 tpd mill starts up, eventually ramping the operation to more than 200,000 oz of gold per year. Marmato Upper appears to break even at roughly $2,654/oz. When gold rose above that level in 2025, Aris raised mill throughput by 19%, and we began to see meaningful production growth. Higher output will help reduce costs at Marmato Upper, but its impact on corporate AISC will be limited because Marmato Upper accounts for only about 10% of total gold production. The real driver of lower Marmato AISC will be the startup of Marmato Lower. According to the 2022 pre-feasibility study based on a $1,600/oz gold assumption, the expanded Marmato project carries an AISC of $1,003/oz. A simple regression of historical AISC against realized gold prices suggests that for every $1,000/oz increase in gold, Marmato AISC rises about $285/oz. At the Q3 2025 realized price of $3,494/oz, that implies an AISC of roughly $1,543/oz—almost $1,400/oz lower than the reported Q3 2025 AISC. Therefore, the startup of Marmato Lower is expected to lead to a major reduction in mine-site AISC.

My back-of-the-envelope estimate shows that once total production reaches 500,000 oz per year, Aris’ corporate AISC would be under $1,600/oz if gold remains at the Q3 2025 level, and no more than about $2,170/oz at the spot gold price of $4,150/oz. On top of that, the rise in annual production from the current 292,000-oz run rate to 500,000 oz will greatly dilute corporate fixed costs; and the CMP know-how gained at Segovia can be applied at Marmato, creating a steep learning curve there and meaningful cost savings. All else equal, I’m confident Aris will be far more efficient in the foreseeable future than it is today.

The upside

At The Natural Resources Hub, we don't build our investment thesis solely on rising gold prices; we therefore focus on factors the company, as a mine operator, controls. Besides gold bullishness and margin expansion from cost cutting, Aris Mining stock's upside will primarily be determined by two drivers: rerating and production growth.

Rerating

Aris Mining is currently valued at $111/oz of in-situ gold-equivalent resources, implying a 2.4× rerating potential to the “ex-Agnico, ex-Lundin Gold, and ex-Alamos” peer-group average of $260/oz AuEq, or a 5× rerating potential to the broader group average of $540/oz AuEq, as illustrated in Figure 17. Although I noted earlier that the EV/Production metric may be less indicative when comparing gold miners, Aris’s valuation of $8,193/ozpa still suggests a 2× rerating potential relative to the group average of $16,447/ozpa, as shown in Figure 18.

In terms of P/NAV, Aris Mining currently trades at 0.58× its net asset value based on technical reports that use gold-price assumptions ranging from $1,600 to $3,000/oz. I estimate that its P/NAV would decline to 0.23× under a $4,150/oz gold scenario. In the gold-mining industry, intermediate producers typically trade at 0.8–1.2× NAV, while senior producers trade at 1.0–1.5× NAV. It is therefore fair to conclude that Aris is significantly undervalued. Accordingly, I believe there is substantial hidden rerating potential in Aris Mining, ranging from 2× to as high as 5×.

Production growth

Aris Mining is executing a long-term plan to become a 1 Mozpa gold producer in Latin America. As discussed earlier (Figure 12), the company is on track to deliver 2× production growth from its 2024 base by 2027, following the start of commercial production at Marmato Lower, and more than 4× production growth from the 2024 base by 2031 by bringing Toroparu and Soto Norte on stream. The combination of a bullish gold price environment and flat or lower AISC should significantly amplify this production growth into industry-leading profit expansion. Between rerating and organic production (and profit) growth, I expect Aris Mining to deliver approximately (2–5) × 4 = 8–20× returns over the next 6–7 years.

The Aris Mining team has a track record of acquisitive growth. While this analysis focuses on organic growth, we should not rule out the possibility that the company pursues M&A to accelerate its expansion. For example, it would be logical for Aris Mining to consider acquiring Collective Mining (CNL), particularly its Apollo discovery, which is located immediately adjacent to Marmato (Figure 19)—though Collective Mining’s executive chairman, Ari Sussman, may not sell it cheaply. It would make perfect sense to unitize the Apollo discovery at Guayabales with Marmato to create a 20+ Moz gold district, over which Aris Mining could apply its CMP expertise at scale. The unitized Marmato–Guayabales district could serve as a cornerstone asset if Aris Mining intends to grow into a senior gold producer—much like Nevada Gold Mines for Newmont and Barrick, Canadian Malartic for Agnico Eagle Mines, Olimpiada for Polyus, or Geita for AngloGold Ashanti (AU). Additionally, Sussman—a serially successful mine builder with deep experience in Colombia—fits naturally into Aris Mining’s long-term executive succession planning and stands out as a strong potential successor to Woodyer when the time comes.

Catalysts

There are several near- and medium-term catalysts that can drive a rerating in Aris Mining and enable its production growth.

  • The acquisition of the remaining 49% interest in Soto Norte from Mubadala is expected to close by early December 2025. While the market has mostly shrugged at the deal because of perceived permitting uncertainty, it is highly impactful—not only because it adds 25% to proven and probable reserves and 17% to measured, indicated, and inferred resources (Figure 9), but also because these in-situ ounces were acquired at exceptionally low cost (see below).
  • At Segovia, the plant is expected to reach full capacity in Q4 2025, pushing production close to 300,000 ozpa, a 14% increase over Q3 2025.
  • At Marmato, first gold from the Lower Mine is expected in H2 2026, followed by a ramp-up through H1 2027 to roughly 200,000 ozpa.
  • At Toroparu, a prefeasibility study is anticipated in October 2026, with an FID targeted for November 2026. This will expand Aris Mining into Guyana—its second Latin American jurisdiction—adding meaningful geographic diversification.
  • Finally, a Soto Norte permit potentially around mid-2028 could remove what is widely regarded as the biggest overhang on the company. Apparently, inclusion in the S&P/TSX Composite Index doesn’t mean the business is spared from being misunderstood.

Risks

Permitting Soto Norte

The greatest uncertainty surrounding Aris Mining is whether the Soto Norte project can be permitted—and, if so, when.

ANLA previously rejected an earlier version of environmental permit application for Soto Norte. On January 19, 2021, ANLA denied all reconsideration requests on procedural grounds, citing insufficient information, thereby confirming its earlier order from October 2, 2020, which shelved the application. That was a setback although the operator (now Aris Mining) has the right to resubmit an EIA. In March 2025, the Colombian Ministry of Environment and Sustainable Development (Ministerio de Ambiente y Desarrollo Sostenible) established a Temporary Reserve Area (TRA) in the Santurbán region, where the Soto Norte project is located. The TRA allows the ministry to evaluate water resources in the area and temporarily suspends the issuance of environmental licenses for two years, until March 2027. At the time of the announcement, the market appeared to interpret the TRA as effectively rendering Soto Norte unpermittable.

Aris management has remained adamant that the project is permitable. During the consultation process leading up to the TRA, the company continued advancing environmental and technical studies for a redesigned PFS. To begin with, Soto Norte is neither in the Santurbán Páramo nor in its buffer zone, and Páramo water flows are separate from those of Soto Norte (Figure 20). In September 2025, Aris released a completely-redesigned PFS with a reduced scale and a smaller environmental footprint, mitigating main environmental concerns and making Soto Norte significantly more permittable. “The Project plans to reuse, recycle, and return approximately 96.5% of the Project’s total water requirements back to the environment, resulting in a net water use estimated at 3.5%.” Importantly, Aris plans to dedicate more than 20% of the 3,500-tpd mill capacity to local community miners, replicating the CMP model already used successfully at Segovia, replacing informal mills that pollute waterways with safe, licensed processing, and helping the Colombian government expand its tax base and collect revenues that would otherwise be difficult to realize.

On October 30, 2025, Aris Mining appointed Brigitte Baptiste, a prominent Colombian environmentalist, to its board of directors. She previously served as director of the Alexander von Humboldt Institute for Research on Biological Resources, which helped define the boundaries of the Santurbán Páramo. Baptiste would not have agreed to join the board if she believed Soto Norte was unpermittable.

Equally significant, on November 19, 2025, Aris announced that it had reached a settlement and termination agreement with the Colombian government to end the arbitration related to the Marmato and Segovia mines that Gran Colombia Gold Corp. had originally brought before the International Centre for Settlement of Investment Disputes. The settlement spared the Colombian government potentially $350 million in payments to Aris. In return, the government now has clear legal obligations to provide security for Aris’ operations, with commitments from the Ministry of Mines to help Aris form CMPs in the Marmato Upper mine, the army, the District Attorney’s office, and the environmental agency. The agreement is with the state of Colombia for a ten-year term, meaning its protections extend beyond the next presidential election on May 31, 2026. The settlement is believed to have created significant goodwill within government agencies.

Earlier, Aris Mining played a leading role in the rescue of 23 workers—18 from a CMP partner and 5 Aris employees—during the La Reliquia mine shaft collapse. This strengthened Aris’s reputation as a responsible, community-minded operator, helped Bogotá avoid bad press, and built goodwill as well.

The goodwill generated from the arbitration settlement, combined with the redesigned PFS, gave Woodyer the confidence to buy the remaining 49% of Soto Norte. Given his business acumen, Woodyer would never have spent $80 million on an asset he doubted could be permitted.

Aris plans to submit the environmental license application for Soto Norte to ANLA in May 2026. I believe Soto Norte is very likely to be permitted. If so, Aris Mining may have pulled off 2025's best transaction in the mining patch. In just 3 ½ years, Aris increased its stake in the 10.9-Moz Soto Norte from 0% to 100%, ending up owning what Eike Batista's mining company, AUX, purchased in 2011 for $1.5 billion:

  • 2022: Aris paid Mubadala $100 million in cash for a 20% operating interest and an option for an additional 30% through exploration capital commitment, implying a transaction metric of $46/oz.

  • May 2024: Aris bought an additional 31% for ~$89 million, paying $26/oz.

  • November 2025: Aris acquired the remaining 49% for $80 million and terminated the Mubadala stream, paying only $15/oz—an extremely low transactional metric.

Toroparu

On October 28, 2025, Aris released a positive PEA on the Toroparu project in Guyana. Judging from the encouraging results, Toroparu appears to carry relatively low execution risk. It is a high-grade (1.3–1.6 g/t gold) open-pit project requiring $820 million in initial capital expenditures, which Aris should be able to self-fund. Aris management is familiar with this geological type of gold deposit from their Endeavour days. Guyana is also one of the top-ranked mining jurisdictions in the world. Guyana is literally West African Birimian geology without the resource nationalism and coup d’états.

The Toroparu prefeasibility study will not be released until October 2026. However, there is good reason for optimism. Toroparu is very similar to the nearby Oko West deposit operated by G Mining Ventures (GMINF), albeit with a lower stripping ratio. In April 2025, G Mining released a robust feasibility study for Oko West, reporting an after-tax IRR of 27% and a 2.9-year payback.

There is a possibility that Aris management may be forced to sell the company too soon thus cutting short its organic growth and the value that would otherwise accrue to shareholders. A plausible acquirer may be Agnico Eagle Mines, which already owns approximately 14.99% of Collective Mining with participation rights in future financings. Agnico may attempt to acquire both Collective Mining and Aris Mining to consolidate the entire Guayabales–Marmato district (Figure 19). Buying Aris would give Agnico access to its CMP expertise, which could be important as Agnico enters Colombia as an operator. That said, I view an early sale as a low-probability outcome. Agnico is likely to wait until Guayabales is permitted before making a move, which is at least four to five years away. By that time, Aris will likely be a much larger and financially stronger company. It has already reduced net debt from $268 million at year-end 2024 to $64 million as of September 30, 2025, and is on track to have a very strong balance sheet.

Investor takeaways

I recently presented Jaguar Gold (OTCQX:JAGGF) as a still-undervalued gold producer. However, Jaguar is a special-situation investment play—a turnaround from an environmental disaster—so I don’t plan to stay in it much longer once the operational recovery is achieved. If you’re searching for an idea with years of profitable growth ahead that still trades at a deep undervaluation, I’d point to Aris Mining.

The all-star team at Aris Mining is quietly building the next major gold producer in South America, targeting 1 million ounces a year within six years—four times today’s output. They have assembled four Tier-1 assets to support that goal.

If those assets were in Ontario, the company would likely trade at several times its current market cap. Instead, they sit in Colombia and Guyana, which explains the deep undervaluation. As this management team continues to advance the projects toward higher production and lower costs, a rerating seems almost unavoidable. In a strong gold market, few stories offer the same blend of high-quality assets and a proven management track record. Aris Mining remains one of the most compelling risk-reward opportunities in the sector.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UDR: Steady, High-Yielding, Well Managed, And Undervalued
data.description:
This appears to be a pretty good time to invest in REITs (real estate investment trusts). The most recent earnings season results saw the biggest jump in REIT earnings since 2022. According to Nareit's T-Tracker report, aggregate FFO (funds from operations) jumped 17.3% YoY (year over year), to just under $21 billion, while FFO per share jumped 13.5%, to $0.80.

Few were surprised when the Fed cut the prime rate to 3.75% today, and 68% of investors expect further rate cuts in 2026, to somewhere between 2.75% and 3.50%. That's up to 125 bps (basis points) below its current level as of this writing.

The average REIT currently yields 3.92%, highly competitive with the "no-risk" range. This is a big change from the past 3 years, when the prime was much higher than the average REIT yield. As the prime rate continues dropping, REIT dividends will probably look more and more appealing to income investors.

From a technical standpoint, the peak in share price of the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ) in December 2021 was very likely the crest of a wave 3, stretching back to 2008. VNQ shares then pulled back to an October 2023 low of $72.34, which it has held on every pullback since then. So $72.34 was likely the wave 4 low. If this analysis is correct, VNQ should reach about $129 in wave 5, which would be 45% above its current price of $89.22. No way of knowing how long that will take, but there is a lot of head room.

There seems to be a significant valuation gap for publicly-traded REITs, according to a November 24 NAREIT article, which reads in part:

REITs provide investors with access to well-located, high-quality, institutional-grade properties managed by best-in-class operators. Across the four traditional property types, U.S. public equity REITs have enjoyed occupancy rates akin to or higher than their private real estate counterparts. REITs have also continued to maintain a pricing advantage. The combination of these factors presents a compelling buying opportunity for real estate investors that appreciate good value.

Investors simply have not rewarded REITs for their stellar operating performance over the past 3 or 4 years. Perhaps that is about to change.

According to a recent article by REITers Digest,

The most significant gap is in apartment REITs, where high-quality REITs continue to trade at significant discounts relative to appraisal rates. All else held equal, this means these companies present a compelling investment opportunity or the appraisal market is displaced.

This article will examine the metrics for a large-cap Apartment REIT that I recently designated as a Buried Treasure.

Meet the company

UDR Inc. (UDR) is headquartered in Highlands Ranch, Colorado, and was founded in 1972. The company has a portfolio of about 60,000 apartment homes in 21 markets, averaging an excellent 96.6% occupancy. The upper northeast U.S. corridor from Boston to Richmond accounts for 40% of the company's NOI (net operating income), with 35% coming from the west coast, and the remaining 25% from the sunbelt states of Texas, Florida, Tennessee, and Colorado. With a market cap of $12.7 billion as of this writing, UDR ranks as the 5th-largest of the Apartment REITs.

The company offers a wider range of price points for tenants than its peers, where rental rate differential is defined as the difference between first-quartile and 3rd-quartile rent. The peer group includes:

  • Avalon Bay (AVB)
  • Camden Property Trust (CPT)
  • Mid-America Apartments (MAA)
  • Essex Property Trust (ESS)
  • Equity Residential (EQR) and
  • Independence Realty Trust (IRT)

UDR leans more toward B-quality properties, which enjoy more stable occupancy. In hard times, affluent apartment dwellers tend to switch from class A to class B accommodations, and in good times, the least affluent tenants tend to upgrade from class C to class B.

In all 3 of the company's geographic segments,

  • the majority of holdings are B-quality, and
  • the majority of holdings are suburban rather than urban

with the skew strongest in the sunbelt in both cases.

Over the past 7 years, UDR has successfully rolled out cost-saving and revenue-enhancing innovations in 5 areas:

  • Ancillary income from parking, package lockers, pet fees, etc.
  • Site-level efficiencies to reduce cost, including unmanned communities and group purchasing
  • Foundational technologies such as SmartHome Tech, CRM, Data Hub, self-guided tours, maintenance tech, and resident app
  • Pricing enhancements, including unit level and amenities
  • Streamlining resident move-in process.

Over the coming years, the company's innovations will tilt toward:

  • Harnessing data including AI for resident and prospect engagement, cyber security, prospect screening, deal identification and underwriting, and staff productivity
  • Enhancing customer experience for higher satisfaction, increased lifetime revenue per resident, higher retention, lower vacancy rates, improved pricing, and margin expansion
  • Community wifi allowing seamless access for residents while reducing emissions
  • Fraud screening AI assisted, to facilitate higher collection rates, lower eviction and turnover rates, and lower vacancy loss.

UDR has successfully reduced resident turnover by 7% over the past 11 quarters, compared to the peer group average of 3.9%, an edge of 310 bps, which equates to about $11 million worth of increased cash flow.

The company drives value creation in 6 ways:

  • Acquisitions targeting NOI growth 10 - 15% above market in first 3 years of ownership
  • Development targeting 6% weighted average projected stabilized yield
  • Redevelopment targeting NOI enhancing improvements that lead to IRR around 15%
  • Debt and Preferred Equity generating 10-15% IRR by providing capital to operators and third-party developers
  • Joint Ventures sourcing attractively priced capital
  • Platform and Innovation improving customer experience through digitalization, centralization, and advanced AI.

Macroeconomic tailwinds

There are 4 distinct factors that favor continued outpeformance by Apartment REITs:

  1. Ongoing U.S. housing shortage
  2. Increased propensity to rent
  3. Housing as a necessity, not a discretionary expense, and
  4. Better long-term NOI growth and lower capex (capital expenditures) than most REIT sectors

Since 2010, the gap between housing units produced versus households formed has increased by 30%. People are renting longer now than in the 1970s. They are waiting 10 years longer to get married (average age 32 versus 22) and 9 years longer to buy their first home (average age 38 versus 29), so they are remaining single and renting for longer periods.

Propensity to rent has gone up 800 bps among the 35-44 age cohort over the TTM (trailing 12 months), with similar but slightly smaller increases in the 45-54 and even the 55-64 age groups.

The high cost of home ownership has made renting much more attractive over the past 4 years. It is currently almost 60% less expensive to rent than to buy a home, according to figures from Moody's, the National Association of Realtors, and Zillow.

Quarterly Results

Here are highlights of UDR's most recent quarterly returns.

Q3 Q3 YTD YTD
Metric 2025 2024 % Change 2025 2024 % Change
Revenues $432 M $420 M +2.9% $1.28 B $1.25 B +2.4%
Operating Income $76.4 M $69.9 M +9.3% $276 M $218 M +26.6%
NOI $43.1 M $23.1 M +86.6% $165 M $101 M +63.4%
Income per Share $0.12 $0.06 +100.0% $0.46 $0.28 +64.3%
Cash From Ops -- -- -- $642 M $636 M +0.9%

Source: UDR 10-Q for Q3 2025

All the numbers moved in the right direction, with modest growth in total revenue, healthy growth in operating income, and spectacular growth in NOI and income per share. The main difference in NOI from Q3 2024 lies in a swing of about $16 million in income from unconsolidated entities. Income per share also reflects a slight decrease in the number of shares outstanding, as the statement below shows.

Growth metrics

Here are the 3-year growth figures for FFO (funds from operations), and TCFO (total cash from operations). All these metrics show steady, plodding, COW-like growth.

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025* 3-year CAGR
FFO (millions) $758 $863 $812 $888 --
FFO Growth % -- 13.8 (-5.9) 9.4 4.04%
FFO per share $2.01 $2.33 $2.48 $2.53 --
FFO per share growth % -- 15.9 6.4 2.0 5.92%
TCFO (millions) $820 $833 $877 $962 --
TCFO Growth % -- 1.6 5.3 9.7 4.07%

Source: Seeking Alpha Premium, iREIT+Hoya Capital, and author calculations

* projected

Meanwhile, here is how the stock price has done over the past 3 twelve-month periods, compared to the REIT average as represented by the Vanguard Real Estate ETF (VNQ).

Metric 2022 2023 2024 2025 3-yr CAGR
UDR share price Dec. 9 $39.89 $35.37 $44.89 $34.91 --
UDR share price Gain % -- (-11.3) 12.5 (-1.3) (-3.28)%
VNQ share price Dec. 9 $85.83 $83.64 $95.88 $88.87 --
VNQ share price Gain % -- (-2.6) 14.6 (-7.3) 0.87%

Source: MarketWatch.com and author calculations

It has been a rough 3 years for REITs, with the VNQ rewarding investors by only 0.87% plus dividend yield, but UDR's shares have underperformed even that modest pace, returning (-3.28)% on shares, and a total return slightly above zero when dividend yield is added in.

Balance sheet metrics

Here are the key metrics for the company's bond-rated balance sheet. The company's solid debt ratio of 28% is slightly better than the REIT average of 30%. Debt/EBITDA is slightly elevated at 5.6, while only 2.3% of the company's debt is held at variable rates.

Company Debt Ratio Debt/EBITDA Var. Bond
UDR 28% 5.6 2.3% BBB+
Apartment REITs 25% 4.9 3.2% --
REIT average 30% 5.2 2.9% --

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

UDR owes approximately $5.05 billion in long-term debt, over against $36.2 million in cash and equivalents and $10.6 billion in assets. Maturities for the next 4 years are quite favorable, at less than 10% of the total each year. Although maturities tick up significantly in 2029 and thereafter, the weighted average interest rate for 2031 - 2033 is ultra-low, under 3.0%. This contributes to the sector-best overall weighted average interest rate of just 3.4% for the entire debt. The average term to maturity is favorable, at 4.6 years.

Slightly over $1 billion of UDR's debt is secured. Properties securing debt as collateral account for 12% of the company's NOI.

Dividend metrics

UDR has not cut its dividend a single time since the Great Financial Crisis of 2009. UDR's dividend yield is 79 bps above the Apartment REIT average, and 100 bps above the REIT average. Nevertheless, the payout of 70% is sustainable, and the Seeking Alpha Quant system rates the dividend safety at C-, leaning slightly toward rewarding investors versus reinvesting income, but not to the point of recklessness. The dividend coverage ratio is 25.5%. The 3-year dividend growth rate is solid but not spectacular, resulting in a Dividend Score well above average.

Company Div. Yield 3-yr Div. Growth Div. Score Payout Div. Safety
UDR 4.92 4.51% 5.62 70% C-
Apartment REITs 4.13 8.20% 5.23 62% C
REIT average 3.92 4.20 4.43 67% C

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, Seeking Alpha Premium, and author calculations

Dividend Score projects the Yield three years from now, on shares bought today, assuming the Dividend Growth rate remains unchanged. Simply put, as a cash cow, UDR is dependable and lucrative.

Valuation metrics

UDR presents the perfect combination for value investors: above-average yield coupled with below-average price/FFO and a hefty discount to NAV. Cheap REITs tend to stay cheap, so UDR price gains may lag the REIT sector somewhat.

Company Div. Score Price/FFO '25 Premium to NAV 3-yr FFO Growth*
UDR 5.62 13.8 (-24.2)% 2.2%
Apartment avg 5.23 15.1 (-20.4)% 2.6%
REIT average 4.43 18.8 (-2.8)% 6.2%

Source: iREIT+Hoya Capital, and author calculations

*Projected

What could go wrong?

UDR operates in just 3 geographic pockets of the U.S. Regional or national economic downturns, upticks in unemployment, or regional migration outflows may affect the company's revenues. Continued declines in mortgage interest rates may make home ownership more attractive, and renting less so. Inflation, particularly in building materials and other development costs, may affect the company's return on building and redevelopment efforts.

Investor's bottom line

Though not a spectacular performer, UDR pays a nice, reliable dividend about 100 bps above the current no-risk rate. The company is well-managed and efficiently operated, and growing slowly but steadily, with strong tenant retention. It is also priced favorably, from both an NAV and FFO standpoint. Although pressure on share price would appear to be upward, the company may underperform on capital gain. Still, from a value and income investor's standpoint, UDR is clearly a Buy.

The Seeking Alpha Quant ratings system and iREIT+Hoya Capital both concur.

However, Zacks, CFRA, and LSEG all rate the company a Hold, while TipRanks rates it a Moderate Buy, with a $41.11 target. Of the 23 Wall Street analysts covering the firm, 15 rate the company a Hold, while 7 rate it a Buy or Strong Buy, and one rates it a Sell. The average analyst price target for UDR is $40.52, implying 15.6% upside in the share price.

But remember, the opinion that matters most is yours. Because it's your money.

data.title: ASML: Don't Sell The King Of EUV Lithography
data.description:
ASML Holding (ASML) stock has woken up in recent months after being in a rut. I only wrote about this company twice. Once in October of 2024 and another time in early February 2025. My first article was a Strong Buy rating, and the second was a downgrade to Buy. In those articles, I argued that the stock could reach $1,000 within 2 years or so (from the low $700s). I wasn't expecting it to happen in less than a year, though. With ASML stock now over $1,100, I believe the short-term upside is much more limited, with the valuation being fair at best now.

Don't get me wrong. ASML is still an amazing company with virtually no real competition in the EUV lithography market (and a strong position in the deep ultraviolet lithography market, with an estimated 90% market share), and it's highly profitable, with more growth ahead. The stock is also in an uptrend right now, so there are real reasons to consider buying it. Despite those positives, the current valuation leads me to give ASML stock a Hold rating right now.

My Original Bull Case On ASML

I originally gave ASML stock a Strong Buy rating because it was an amazing company (arguably the best in the world) at a reasonable price. At the time, analysts' estimates for 2026 were $33.48 per share, and a 30x multiple on that EPS would get you to a share price of $1,004.40. Considering that the share price was in the low $700s, that seemed like a good deal.

I also talked about the company's high profitability, and in my second article, I discussed the CapEx boom for chip spending. These positives are still true today.

However, since my first article, the stock has returned 57.8%, and EPS estimates have actually fallen (EPS for 2026 is now expected to be $30.31). This is a double whammy that makes the stock less attractive now.

ASML Is Still A Beast

Despite my rating downgrade, ASML is still an amazing company that has generated €32.2B in revenue in the past 12 months. After all, it has relatively high profit margins of 29.4% for the past 12 months, and its margins have been slowly trending upward in the past 10 years. ASML's net income of 29.4% gets an “A” grade on Seeking Alpha, as it's much higher than the 4.98% sector median and higher than its five-year average of 28.13%.

Further, despite having a low debt/equity ratio of 14.2% (€2.705B of debt compared to €18.994B of equity), its return on equity sits at 53.9% for the past 12 months. Its debt/equity has actually been falling over the years, which would negatively impact ROE. For example, at the end of 2022, its debt/equity ratio was 50.9%, based on debt of €4.487B compared to €8.811B.

Either way, ASML has managed to maintain a high ROE. Plus, the fact that its ROE is consistently high—and on an uptrend—shows just how strong the company's moat is. The 53.9% ROE gets an A+ grade from Seeking Alpha. The sector median is 6.6%.

Financial figures aside, which I can talk about all day, ASML is the only company in the world that manufactures Extreme Ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines, which are necessary to make the most advanced chips. Its EUV machines are currently “high NA,” with NA standing for numerical aperture. And this larger NA allows more complex chips.

Not only is ASML the only EUV lithography company in the world, but management has also stated that hyper-NA machines will be on the market in the longer term, which will allow for even more advanced chips.

When asked about hyper NA and when it will be available, Cristophe Fouquet, CEO of ASML, said, “For us, it's not necessarily a difficult product. We need our customers to need it as well. And if you look at the roadmap of our customer, I think the need for hyper NA will be somewhere between 2032 and 2035.”

Did you catch that? Management stated that hyper NA isn't necessarily difficult for the company. There's just no demand for it yet. But eventually, there will be. And when the demand comes, ASML will be ready and will likely still be ahead of the competition as a result. I'm not sure how many other companies can confidently claim that hyper NA isn't difficult for them. With ASML's track record, I trust management's word.

A Valuation Update

You may be able to tell from the chart below that ASML's normalized P/E ratio near 41-42x is around an average range when compared to the past few years.

Seeking Alpha states that ASML's five-year average non-GAAP P/E ratio (TTM) is 40.85x, which is almost in line with its current valuation. When looking at it from that lens, it looks fairly valued.

When judging it based on its NTM P/E of 37.05x, it looks elevated, since the three-year average forward P/E ratio is 33.05x, according to TIKR.com.

So, when comparing its valuation to its historic multiples, ASML stock is fairly valued at best or just slightly overvalued at worst. Still, that may somewhat seem like the classic “wonderful company at a fair price,” and you might not be wrong in thinking so. But is it a screaming buy? I don't think so.

You also need to consider the possibility of ASML's growth slowing down over time as the company grows. If so, a 40x multiple may prove to be a tad high. Anywhere from 30-35x earnings is a pretty good price to pay for ASML (at the moment), in my opinion.

Can ASML's Dominance Be Challenged?

ASML currently holds a monopoly in the EUV industry, but it might not be that way forever. An article from trendforce.com talked about how Huawei is testing a laser-induced discharge plasma (LDP) system, which is different than ASML's laser-produced plasma (LPP). Here's what the article stated:

The tool, believed to be developed by China’s Harbin Provincial Innovation, uses a LDP-based method to generate the critical 13.5nm wavelength EUV light. According to the report, instead of ASML’s laser-blasted tin droplets, this system vaporizes liquid tin between electrodes and converts it into plasma through high-voltage discharge.

However, as Chinese self-media outlet Business Morning on eastmoney.cm explains, where LDP shows its edge over traditional DPP is in operational longevity. Conventional discharge-produced plasma systems suffer from excessive thermal loading on electrodes and severe corrosion issues. LDP could reportedly alleviates these problems by offloading some of the initial heating work to lasers.

Nonetheless, this is not something easy to achieve, and the article noted that, saying:

Yet even with these improvements, the path ahead remains steep. Within the current technological landscape, LDP still faces challenges in matching the performance and reliability of ASML’s laser-produced plasma approach, Business Morning notes.

The fact is that ASML is way ahead of the game right now and is a trusted supplier. Trust is huge. Not to mention, it will take lots of R&D spending for a company to catch up to ASML, so I don't see competition as an immediate threat. Still, this is something worth monitoring.

The Bottom Line On ASML Stock

ASML remains a powerhouse of a company—arguably the best in the world with its monopoly on EUV lithography machines and its consistently high profitability. On the other hand, the valuation is not at a very attractive level. It's more of a fair/slightly overvalued price, if I had to guess. It doesn't look like a screaming Buy to me anymore, so I am downgrading it to a Hold. If you own it, great. You can still hold it and do well. But I would think twice before adding to a current position.

Besides this, I will keep an eye on China's EUV technology to see if it actually ends up being a threat to ASML. However, with ASML's lead in the industry and management's claim that hyper NA is not necessarily hard for them to achieve, I don't see competition being an issue anytime soon.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Oracle's AI Warning Shot
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

The AI trade hit a speedbump as Oracle fell short on the top line. (0:15) SpaceX gearing up to go public. (1:55) Meta’s new AI team clashing with old guard. (3:04)

Oracle (ORCL) is slumping premarket after posting mixed fiscal Q2 results, even as the company continues to sign major cloud and AI deals. Shares are down more than 10%.

Oracle reported adjusted earnings of $2.26/share, above the $1.64 consensus. Revenue rose 14% to $16.06B, but that was just shy of expectations.

Cloud revenue — infrastructure plus applications — climbed 36% to $8B, narrowly missing estimates. Infrastructure was the standout, up 66% in constant currency to $4.1B, while application revenue rose 11% to $3.9B. Fusion Cloud ERP grew 18% to $1.1B.

A key metric, remaining performance obligations, surged 438% to $523B, boosted by new commitments from big names including Meta (META) and NVIDIA (NVDA).

Seeking Alpha analysts Trapping Value said Oracle's miss is a “genuine miss.”

“The company once again pointed to RPO, which until one quarter back, no one knew or cared about,” they said.

Oracle also booked a $2.7B pre-tax gain from selling its stake in Ampere, lifting both GAAP and non-GAAP EPS. The sale follows SoftBank’s agreement earlier this year to buy Ampere for $6.5B.

CTO Larry Ellison said Ampere was no longer strategic as Oracle shifts to “chip neutrality,” working with all major CPU and GPU suppliers. He emphasized that Oracle will keep buying Nvidia GPUs but wants the flexibility to deploy any chip customers require.

“ORCL gave the AI bull run a warning shot," Trapping Value said. “Yes, despite all that capex spend, revenues can miss. We think margin compression is already in the works and likely accelerates as revenue growth slows down.”

Elon Musk has confirmed that reports about a potential SpaceX (SPACE) IPO are accurate.

Replying to an X post from Ars Technica’s Eric Berger, Musk said “As usual, Eric is accurate,” effectively endorsing Berger’s view that SpaceX is positioning itself to go public.

Berger has argued the company could move ahead soon, saying an offering would help support $30B+ in capital needs tied to AI-driven projects such as space-based data centers and lunar satellite factories.

Recent reports suggest SpaceX could pursue a massive IPO as early as mid-2026, targeting a valuation north of $1T, potentially up to $1.5T, on the strength of Starlink and Starship.

That comes despite Musk recently pushing back on rumors about fundraising and valuation, noting SpaceX has been cash-flow positive for years and mainly uses buybacks to provide liquidity. He said valuation steps are tied to progress with Starlink, Starship, and securing global direct-to-cell spectrum — “arguably the most significant” factor.

Musk has also floated the idea of finding a way for Tesla (TSLA) shareholders to participate in SpaceX equity, acknowledging that going public “despite all the downsides” may eventually make sense.

And an “us-versus-them” split is emerging inside Meta Platforms (META) between the company’s new superintelligence group and longtime lieutenants to Mark Zuckerberg.

The New York Times reports that Alexandr Wang, recently hired to lead Meta’s superintelligence push, has privately disagreed with senior executives including product chief Chris Cox and CTO Andrew Bosworth. Cox and Bosworth reportedly want Wang’s team to use Instagram and Facebook data to train Meta’s next foundational “frontier” model — strengthening feeds and advertising.

But Wang has pushed back, arguing the priority should be catching up to OpenAI (OPENAI) and Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) before folding AI deeper into products.

The Times also reports internal disputes over computing resources: teams working on social-media ranking want more of Meta’s new compute to improve the core business, while the AI lab wants it directed toward model training. Some researchers in the new lab are said to view other Meta executives as too focused on incremental business gains rather than the group’s ambition to build superintelligence.

One flashpoint: sources told the Times that Bosworth was recently asked to cut $2B from next year’s Reality Labs budget, with the money redirected to Wang’s team.

Meta spokesman Dave Arnold disputed that characterization, saying there are no clashes among Wang, Cox, and Bosworth, and denying that $2B had been shifted. He added that next year’s budget is not yet final.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

A report says Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan pursued deals that benefitted him.

David Ellison addresses Warner Bros. shareholders in a letter to accept Paramount's bid.

Chinese AI unicorns MiniMax and Zhipu are said to be eyeing imminent Hong Kong IPOs.

In premarket trading, stock index futures (SPX) (INDU) (US100:IND) are lower, with the momentum names dragged down by Oracle. Treasury yields (US2Y) (US10Y) are lower, with the short end pressured by the Fed’s announcement that it would be buying T-bills.

Today’s economic calendar

8:30 am – Weekly Initial Jobless Claims

data.title: Kellanova: A Global Snacks Engine Being Cashed Out At Full, But Not Frothy, Value
data.description:
Mars’ $83.50 all-cash offer puts the value of the snacks-led transformation and emerging markets growth of Kellanova into sharp relief. With growth in developed markets slowing down and FX pressure remaining, I do not see much upside left for public investors and hence view the stock only as a hold-to-close arbitrage opportunity.

Business Quality: Brand Architecture as Shock Absorber

Kellanova (K) is the snacks-focused successor to the legacy Kellogg Company. Following the spinoff in 2023, the slow-growth cereal business in North America was spun off as WK Kellogg Co, leaving Kellanova the faster-growing engines of the business: Global Snacks, International Cereals, African Noodles, and the North American Frozen & Plant-based Foods businesses. Today, with about thirteen billion dollars in annual net sales, it competes among the big players in the worldwide snacking arena, together with monoliths like Mondelez and Hershey.

The portfolio is much different from the traditional view of a breakfast cereal conglomerate. The snacks business drives the revenue, with Pringles, Cheez-It, Pop-Tarts, Rice Krispies Treats, and RXBAR. International cereal and African noodles form the next growth curve of the business, with Eggo frozen breakfast and MorningStar Farms plant-based meals adding sustainable revenue, but at a lower growth rate. This structure of the brand is important because it gives Kellanova several ways of responding to shocks.

However, where the company truly distinguishes itself is in how it leverages these brands for shock absorption on the inflation and demand cycles. Over the past several years, management was very aggressive about passing along price, absorbing rising costs of ingredients, labor, and logistics, although volumes performed better than several other companies, where there clearly was a large trade-down into private-label products. The reason for this, of course, is partially psychological. Cheez-It, Pringles, and Pop-Tarts are clearly in the “small reward” category: affordable enough that they remain accessible, but distinctive enough that private labels are not ideal substitutes.

The second structural strength lies in the exposure the company has to emerging markets. The Asia-Pac, MEA, and Latin America regions contribute to over one-third of their total sales, but the contribution of these regions, along with the growth, is much higher. This is because the instant noodles, ready-to-eat cereals, and snacks markets in these emerging markets are still in the penetration stage. In the last year, the growth of their net sales, organically, in these regions was in the double digits, although the reported growth was somewhat impacted because of the currency. The Mars acquisition itself is proof of the quality of business. The private confectionery giant signed a deal in 2024 to acquire Kellanova for eighty-three dollars and fifty cents per share in cash, which valued the business at around thirty-six billion dollars. This acquisition will help Kellanova's snacks, cereals, and noodles business, along with Mars's chocolates and gum business, become a powerhouse with billion-dollar brands. Mars is not a short-term player, and it believes that the compound growth of Kellanova will add value for the next several decades.

Positioning for Uncertainty

However, the question is whether Kellanova is structurally ready for a time when consumer attitudes are shifting, the total size of the weight-loss drug market is expanding, and the strength of retailer clout is growing. The short answer is that the company has made genuine strides, but it is not invincible.

On the product line, the management chose not to spin off MorningStar Farms and other higher-protein brands, such as RXBAR, when passion for plant-based nutrition cooled. These platforms give the company the opportunity to go deeper into the “permissible indulgence” or wellness space if the regulators or the healthcare community shift against sugar and fat. Special K cereals and Nutri-Grain bars also offer the opportunity to shift the message toward protein and fiber, abandoning the message of sweetness.

As regards the channel and geography, the emerging market weighting provides diversification. The corporation is less reliant on the U.S. shelf than the typical cereal brand, deriving about half of total revenue from outside of North America. In the Nigerian and Mexican markets, the demand for convenient packaged meals helps mitigate the GLP-1 risk that pervades discussions for U.S. investors.

However, Kellanova is not sheltered from the new environment, either. Weight management aids suppress the drive to eat and the need for snacks for a segment of the purchasing public, and the supermarket industry is exerting pressure on private labels within stressed categories. The strength of the company’s business lies in brand differentiation and innovation, such as the development of bite-sized “Mingles” for Pringles or the launching of Cheez-It in several countries, following the initial successful foray into Mexico.

The spin-off itself could also be seen as a mixed bag. Through the spin-off of WK Kellogg Co, Kellanova shed the slow-growth, high-promotion cereal business in North America. Yet it also raised the bar on consumer confidence-related cyclicality of volumes, given that the business is less about staples for breakfast, and more about discretionary snacking, which could certainly become a headwind if it is a mild recession or, worse, driven by the growing use of GLP-1 drugs.

Financials: What the Numbers Reveal

Financially, the recent history of Kellanova is one that demonstrates substantial margin recovery layered on top of small growth.

In terms of results for 2024, the company recorded about thirteen billion dollars of net sales and finished the inaugural full year as a stand-alone snacks business. Organic net sales recorded low-to-mid single-digit growth, excluding the effect of currency and the Russia exit. More significantly, adjusted gross margin regained the mid-thirty percentage level, and the adjusted operating margin got closer to the fourteen percent level, which the management had envisioned for the new structure.

Cash generation returned with it. Operating cash flow for 2024 was about one point eight billion dollars, and free cash flow around one point one billion dollars, which suggests a mid-single-digit free cash flow yield at pre acquisition prices. The management leveraged this strength to pay about fifty-eight cents per quarter per share on the dividend, with net debt at about two to two-and-a-half times EBITDA. This level of debt, for a consumer staples business, is conservative.

However, the more recent quarterly results reveal a business that not only appears to have finished fixing the margin but is also faced with a less-demanding developed market environment, although growth continues in emerging markets. For a recent quarter, net sales were a little above three point one billion dollars, down about two percent year over year but ahead of expectations, and EPS, or adjusted earnings per share, of around ninety-plus cents, beating estimates. This is primarily driven by effective cost management, slowing commodity price inflation, and supply chain optimization.

However, simultaneously, there are emerging indications of the pressures. In the recent quarterly results, the organic net sales declined about one percent year over year, with price and mix, which had helped to mitigate the loss of volume, only partially offsetting the losses in the regions of North America and Europe, and FX translation also impacting Latin America. However, the adjusted operating profit declined by a low double digit percentage compared to the previous year to maintain support for the brand and shelf space, instead of backing margins. The free cash flow for the initial half of the year also decreased. Overall, the picture that is painted by these figures is that of a business that has managed to regain profitability from the shock of inflation, but is currently undergoing a shift, where it is transitioning from a period where growth was driven by pricing alone, since innovation and emerging markets need to bear more of the burden.

Valuation: What Are You Paying for Quality?

Being at the current trading price of below eighty-three dollars and fifty cents, Kellanova's equity is effectively pegged on the Mars offer. However, it is worth dissecting the stand-alone valuation to derive the determination of whether Mars is paying too much, too little, or the correct price for the industry.

Based on the latest information, the market capitalization for Kellanova is below thirty billion dollars, with the enterprise value of around thirty-five billion dollars. Based on that, the P/E ratio is in the low twenties, the Forward P/E also in the low twenties, and the EV/EBITDA ratio is around fifteen times. EV/Sales is a bit over two times. These are not distressed valuations, indicating a premium over the slowest cereal-based rivals but a discount compared to the best snack and confectionery franchises.

Compared to the peer group – Kraft Heinz, General Mills, Hershey, Tyson, and McCormick – Kellanova appears to be properly valued in the middle of the group. While Kraft Heinz and General Mills, more consumer staples-focused, are trading for lower teens multiples of earnings and around ten times EV/EBITDA, Hershey, with their highly dominant U.S. confectionery business, trades for a high teens EV/EBITDA multiple and a mid-to-high twenties P/E. Tyson, with their commodity cycles in meat, remains lower on both revenue and earnings multiples. McCormick, with their dominance in the spice business, typically remains around the middle teens EV/EBITDA multiple. This puts Kellanova's fifteen times EV/EBITDA and low twenties P/E multiples consistent for the mid-single-digit growth rate, the better margins, and the moderately levered balance sheet.

However, on a basic cross-check using the discounted cash flow analysis, the message is the same. To get from the ballpark figure of the thirteen billion revenue base, with three percent growth, a progression toward the fifteen percent operating margin, the low twenties for the cash taxes, and terminal growth of two to three percent, and using the seven-and-a-half-to-eight percent discount rate, I get the equity valuation range of around seventy-five dollars to around ninety dollars a share. Mars’s offer price of eighty-three fifty is right at the midpoint. Essentially, the public shareholders are getting a fair exit.
