Insiders & Sentiment

Following the drawing up of a clear merger deal, insider activity had remained minimal and was strictly regulated. The more significant insider activity, of course, was the strategic one, where the board accepted the offer. This was an indication that the boards, including management, felt that it was better off realizing the value of the business rather than focusing on growing multiples.

The ownership patterns have kept pace with this. The traditional dividend-focused and long equity funds have progressively decreased their holdings, given the shift in the return profile, which changed from the sum of dividend payments and growth to a single cash flow. Merger arbitrage funds, also known as event-driven strategies, are the new dominant longs, fixing the stock price within a range below eighty-three dollars and fifty cents, earning the spread for the deal close. The short interest is not substantial, given the defensiveness and high likelihood of consummation. The sell-side sentiment has moved toward a mechanical perspective. The target prices are effectively on top of the break-up consideration, and the analyst ratings are centered around a Hold rating. The analyst reports are dominated by the regulatory milestones and the closing date, and not the snack structure thesis. In terms of the sentiment, it is closer to a short-term bond than a typical consumer staples stock.

Catalysts On the Horizon

For the public equity of Kellanova, the driving force is very simple. The necessary approvals have been obtained, and Mars is on track to finalize the acquisition soon. The small difference between the trading price and eighty-three fifty is merely on account of the time-value premium. Once the acquisition is finalized, the equity will be exchanged for money and will subsequently be delisted.

However, within the corporation, there are several catalysts that will remain important for Mars and, by proxy, for how investors could think about similar situations down the line.

One is the execution of innovation platforms like Pringles Mingles and the launch of Cheez-It globally. If these efforts drive real volume growth, versus simply trading consumers through the portfolio, it will prove that the brand platform can continue to compound even in private ownership.

The other is the path of emerging markets. It is also likely that, if the inflation and currency-related challenges faced by the African and Latin American businesses ease, the current double-digit organic growth that is apparent will show up well in the numbers. That will only add to the underlying value of what Mars is buying.

Finally, input costs and logistics also remain an upside lever that is external. A period of steady grains and packaging, combined with steady pricing, would help fuel the next phase of margin growth. These benefits will accrue behind the Mars ownership umbrella, where they will remain private, rather than in a hypothetical stand-alone situation where they perhaps deserve a premium multiple.

Risks: Where Could This Break?

The usual risks for consumer staples are present, but the structural risks are the more interesting ones.

On the demand-facing side, the use of GLP-1 weight management drugs could suppress appetite and the number of snacking occasions for a segment of the population. For a portfolio that is anchored around indulgent treats, even a small, permanent reduction in per-capita snacking could stall volume growth. Kellanova has several of better-for-you brands, but these are not the majority.

The presence of the private-label competitor and other branded players is increasingly forcing the competitive environment. Retailers are becoming more willing to allocate additional shelf space to their own brands when it comes to cereals, crackers, and frozen products, especially if the consumer is looking for better pricing. This means that Kellanova could require higher promotion support, which could harm the margins it so diligently rebuilt.

Volatility of foreign exchange and emerging markets is a constant companion. A weakening currency can offset double-digit organic growth in the AMEA or Latin America sectors. Instability, import bans, or new market rules could also make business even tougher.

Finally, there is always deal risk left, albeit small at this juncture. It is not impossible that a shock or last-minute regulatory, or finance-related, complication could throw this deal off track. But again, the stock price will revert to a fundamentals-based valuation that will likely come out lower than the market, and investors will have to pay for their own uncertainty around GLP-1, FX, and mature market stagnation.

Conclusion: Still Worth the Price?

Kellanova has made the successful transition from a big cereal conglomerate to a focused worldwide snacks and emerging markets engine, and Mars is paying what appears to be a fair industrial price for the engine. Taking growth in developed markets, FX uncertainty around progress in emerging markets, and the eighty-three-fifty all-cash offer effectively capping upside, I view Kellanova at this stage as a position to Hold until closure.

data.title: Adtran: High Debt And Growing Receivables Continuing To Weigh On Stock (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote on ADTRAN Holdings, Inc. (ADTN) in September of this year, when we reiterated our 'Hold' rating in the network and communication platform-oriented outfit. Shares were trading at $9.43 at the time, meaning they have lost just under 7% over the past 11 weeks. Although there were some encouraging trends in the company's third quarter earnings report (released on 11/3/2025) this year, we are maintaining our Hold rating in ADTN until further notice.

Negative earnings and rising debt were key reasons for our caution back in September, despite strong showings in the company's 3 segments (Optical Networking, Subscriber Solutions, and Access & Aggregation) in the second quarter this year. Furthermore, top-line growth was not materially dropping to the bottom line, with management having to issue a private note offering of $175 million to manage its elevated debt.

Investors Remain Sceptical Post Q3 Earnings

Although we saw strong performances once more in all three of ADTRAN's segments in Q3 (from a top-line perspective), investors did not take well to the report, as we will discuss later in the technical section. Although, as mentioned, ADTRAN's three segments managed to grow their top lines by double-digit percentages in Q3, management announced the closing of a $200+ million financing transaction, again to manage the company's debt. Suffice it to say, as we learn below, management wants to sell the premise that ADTRAN can 'grow' its way out of trouble, where momentum can continue to abound on the back of how management has pivoted the company in recent times.

Turning to the quarterly results. ADTRAN reported $279.4 million, reflecting strong year-over-year growth across all 3 revenue categories. This marks the fifth consecutive quarter of sequential growth and fourth consecutive quarter of year-over-year improvement, proof that our portfolio strategy and market positioning are driving sustainable momentum. This consistency underscores the health of our business, continued improvement in market conditions and the progress we are making in strengthening our foundation for the longer-term growth.

Receivables On the Rise

However, top-line growth rates aside, GAAP profit for the third quarter came in at -$10.3 million, receivables increased to $193.8 million, and reported net debt arrived at $149.1 million. Although operating cash flow once more came in positive in the 3 months ($12.2 million), receivables, as mentioned, rose by $13.5 million. This is the problem with trying to protect the balance sheet, but also generate positive cash flow in a negative earnings environment. It can be done temporarily, but usually something has to give.

Therefore, what investors need to contemplate here is how fast the company's growing receivables can be collected so they can be used in tandem to bring down ADTRAN's debt load. This is the key question because the company continues to carry far too much debt when compared to its profitability.

Technical Analysis

From a technical analysis perspective, being chartists, we believe in three premises that guide our investment decisions. The first is that ADTRAN's share-price action discounts everything. What this means is that every piece of known information (valuation, fundamentals, growth rates, profitability, end-market strength, etc.) has already been baked into how shares trade on their respective technical charts. If prices are going up, ADTRAN's fundamentals are bullish, whereas if they are plunging, it means the company's fundamentals are bearish at any given time.

The second is that stock prices tend to move in trends, so it is always in our interest to have our positions aligned with the prevailing trend. To this point, trends tend to persist rather than reverse, which is why it is imperative to always be on the right side of the established trend.

The third premise is that history tends to repeat itself more often than not due to the following reason. Human psychology plays a pivotal role in how stocks trend, as it is a key metric that tends not to change. This is why trendlines (especially on long-term charts) are significant, as they show how investors acted in the past under a given setup. We will return to the above premises throughout the remainder of this article.

Long-Term Technical Chart

On ADTRAN's 20-year chart, the long-term trends of lower highs and lower lows remain intact, confirming the stock remains in a long-term bear market. Although we had stated in recent commentary that shares could feasibly rally back up to the upper trendline (approx. $20 a share) in due time, this would still only constitute a bear-market rally if it were to materialise. Nevertheless, this does not look likely now, given the steep turnaround in ADTRAN's 10-month moving average in recent months, resulting in a falling histogram, demonstrating that the upward trend is weakening. Case in point: ADTRAN's long-term trend remains bearish.

Intermediate 5-Year Chart

On the intermediate 5-year chart, the depicted bearish trendline, which dates back to 2022, provides significant overhead resistance for ADTRAN at approximately the $10 level. Here we turn to one of our above premises mentioned earlier, that 'history tends to repeat itself' in financial markets. Well, as we see below, ADTRAN stock has not been able to punch above intermediate resistance on multiple occasions this year. Therefore, given that buying has been amiss at this resistance point, isn't it likely that the same pattern will play out once more? Remember, the longer trendlines remain in play (whether they offer support or resistance), the more potent they become. Suffice it to say, overcoming $10 a share over the near term looks like a sizable task for ADTRAN, all things remaining equal.

12-Month Technical Chart

On ADTRAN's short-term chart, we see shares presently trying to remain above the 200-day moving average, which has been acting as resistance. Furthermore, the downside gap in early November will further act as overhead resistance above the $9.10 level. Do we see both of these levels being taken out to the upside in due course? Not likely, given the poor volume trends we have seen in ADTRAN, especially since September of this year (announcement relating to the convertible senior notes offering). The notes offering (and how it affected ADTRAN's stock at the time) is a prime example of how the market discounts everything over time (the first technical premise discussed earlier).

Conclusion

To sum up, we are reiterating our Hold rating in ADTRAN, as we see minimal upside potential in the months to come. Bearish technical trends, negative earnings, and a leveraged balance sheet continue to keep buyers away for now. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: FuelCell Energy: Not A Lot Of Faith This Can Be A Winner
data.description:
Investment Thesis

FuelCell Energy (FCEL) I remember being one of the supposed big ones in the green energy wave that occurred a few years ago. Its technology is fascinating and highly scalable, but the production isn't there and neither are the margins right now.

Weighing this with insiders both selling and the company still diluting massive amounts of shares, likely even more so now after the stock rose so sharply in October of this year. I think you’d be better off staying away from the stock for the moment, which is why I’m rating it a sell.

Stock Chart 5-Year Performance

For the price comparison I’ve decided to look at Bloom Energy (BE), Plug Power (PLUG), Ballard Power Systems (BLDP) and Invesco WinderHill Clean Energy ETF (PBW) as they offer similar exposure. All are developing some form of fuel cell but as the chart shows there has been but one winner here.

Bloom Energy has been on a massive run this year as it continues to ramp up production and managed to get both gross, operating and net margins positive on a TTM basis. FCEL, which is today's stock to discuss, is still very far from this goal.

On a YTD basis FCEL is down nearly 30% but did surge from below $4 to nearly $12 per share in a matter of 2 months or so. The industry does have a lot of potential as a viable option for scaling power generation whilst at the same time also reducing the amount of emissions and collecting credits for this. A few years ago it would have been very tough to choose a winner which is why I’ve mostly stayed out of this industry all together. But since some of the stocks have been on such a run this year I figured I’d give it a look. Despite the run, YTD Bloom Energy still looks to be by far the best play here because of its already scaled production and much clearer path towards higher margins, compared to both FCEL and PLUG.

Operations Overview

FCEL I remember being a very talked about company a few years ago as the technology it had seemed revolutionary for the green energy wave as it could help in capturing more carbon emissions and make gas generated electricity far more environmentally friendly. It has since transformed into portraying itself more as a play on data centers and being able to scale with the energy demand that these have.

In the core FCEL is an electrochemical technology company selling a platform of sorts, a platform which basically takes the natural gas inputs, and transforms that into electricity and then is able to capture a large portion of the carbon emissions from this process. Because of the relatively small size and in theory high scalability FCEL could rapidly be deployed along sites where companies are building data centers.

Above is an image depicting the % of sales each segment has contributed over the past 5 years, measured for the Q3 for all. If I would be more granular, the chart would be slightly less dramatic, but this is the reality with FCEL, revenue streams differ heavily from year to year and quarter to quarter. FCEL reports 4 different revenue lines: Product, Service, Generation and Advanced Technology. Out of these the most important for the future is without a doubt “Product” as it relates to the sale of its oxide fuel cells. The uses are not just carbon capture but could be for hydrogen generation and overall power generation as well. The diversified use case is perhaps an understated advantage for FCEL.

As for the remaining three segments, Service relates to revenues generated from mult-year contracts where FCEL basically maintains the performance of the already installed fuel cells, along with monitoring services. These revenues tend to be more stable as they are recurring.

The third segment is Generation which as the name suggests relates to electricity sales that FCEL itself generated as it owns an asset base of power generation. Revenues come on a metered output and again can be relatively stable.

Lastly we have the Advanced Technologies segment which instead revolves around R&D and copiloting fuel cell installations along with general carbon capture and hydrogen projects. Importantly is that a large portion of these revenues are funded by grants. From agreements with companies like ExxonMobil (XOM) for example.

Above I’ve made a graph showing the gross margins for each of the revenue lines, also during the same period as the table before. What immediately jumps out is the steady decline of both product and advanced technologies margins. But on the contrary Generation is posting a strong uptrend, going from negative 66.7% in Q3 2022 to now negative 24% now. Importantly we’d want to see the first segment improve much faster seeing as that will be the main revenue stream over the long-term most likely.

Measured on a 9 month basis for 2025 and 2024 the results actually look a lot better with the combined revenue amount growing 64.22% YoY, most of that coming from increased revenues in Product sales. Again, besides just growth in revenues, FCEL has also delivered a strong gross margin improvement going from negative 39.8% to now negative 19.2% YTD. Most of the cost savings came from the Generation segment. The cited reason in the 10Q is lower costs relating to the Toyota Project.

On the topic of margins, operating margins are deeply negative sitting at (-98%) now but have improved from (-187%) during the first nine months of 2024. I should say that I am using normalized operating losses, meaning I don’t account for the impairment charges the company incurred during 2025 as I believe it does not represent the core operations. The impairment charge in question was described to be about inventory and goodwill along with certain investments the company has previously made.

Impairment expense of $64.5 million for the three months ended July 31, 2025 related to the Company's prior investments in solid oxide technology, including related goodwill and in-process research and development (“IPR&D”) intangible assets, property, plant and equipment and solid oxide inventory. Of the $64.5 million, approximately $42.1 million was related to property, plant and equipment, approximately $9.0 million was related to inventory, approximately $9.3 million was related to IPR&D intangible assets, and approximately $4.1 million was related to goodwill.

During the past few years the cost of SG&A has come down as well quite significantly and is now more in line with R&D expenses as a % of the overall OPEX. This is a positive sign seeing as the company has also grown its headcount from 316 in 2020 to as the last report 584. More normalized OPEX lines from here on will make valuing the stock a lot easier.

Weak Margins & High Dilution

The path to profitability is on every investor's mind right now in FCEL. In the past few years the company has done a solid job of raising both gross and operating margins as I’ve discussed earlier here. The issue seems to stem more now from actually scaling and being able to deliver more fuel cells.

Generation is by far the largest segment right now in terms of backlogs. Quite disappointingly is the fact that the product segment is barely seeing any rise in backlogs even as the company tries to expand in data centers. This expansion is not a new thing, so some effect should have been seen if this was an appealing proposal for companies. The lack thereof is a worrying sign.

Currently I don't see a viable path to profitability for the company. It has continued to underperform the market expectations and even if revenues have risen FCEL continues to lack the capability of bringing costs down. On one hand you have quite constrained growth in the OPEX lines, but since this is a company planning to scale off of fuel cell sales and that cost item is still largely in the negative, -11.85% last quarter and with years or barely any sales at all I think a lot of pessimism here is warranted.

Because of the lack of profitability so far the company continues to resort to equity financing as its main source of capital to help sustain operations. With a current cash pile of $174 million, growing from $148 million last year FCEL should be able to bear another 5 - 6 quarters of normalized OPEX. Just YoY the diluted share number grew by 45.7%, going from 16.772 million to 24.441 million. Seeing as the last report ended on July 31 and the price surged in the months after that I’d expect the next report from the company to show a serious amount of further dilution as well. We’ll know more on December 18th when the next report is set to come.

The supposed 30% annual operating expense cuts I don't see as realistic, from what exact line of expense would that come from? Is the company going to fire a lot of people or cut back on R&D spending and lose out on their competitive edge? Neither is good so this statement from management unfortunately doesn't hold a lot of weight in my opinion. Unless they can get to the core issue which is the costs of essentially all their revenue items then there isn’t much of a positive case here to be made.

The Tough Reality

The tough reality is that FCEL is nowhere close to profitability today than it was a few years ago. The difference instead has a massive amount of dilution and a backlog which seems to be depleting and worked through, but not actually being able to translate into even gross profits for the business.

On a Price/Sales or Price/Book metric the stock doesn't look overvalued and to some extent would even suggest an undervaluation based on the book value. However, because of the restructuring plan the company announced last year, which so far seems to have resulted in the asset base falling, the valuation that seems cheap is hiding the fact that asset values is likely to decrease further, causing the multiple to rise.

Share-based compensation exists in almost every company, but $8.6 million YTD seems excessive seeing as the business is not even producing a gross profit on a reliable basis yet. It has slowed down YoY but without it operating cash flows would have been about 8 - 10% higher. This isn’t the time to be rewarding shares when the company is still not performing.

Based on some recent insider reports it doesn't seem that management either has a lot of value in holding shares. The CFO for instance has been selling almost as soon as receiving shares, holding about 10,138 shares right now. He received about 1,167 worth of shares and sold twice for a combined total of 688 shares at an average price of $8.87.

Weighing all of the negatives like disappointing overall margin development the past 5 years, massive share dilution and a depleting backlog which isn't growing, the stock does fit the criteria of being a sell.

The Full Picture

The fact of the matter is this, FuelCell has a very solid product but it has been unable to scale this over the past 5 years and is now still struggling with raising margins. There have been some improvements in certain revenue types, but overall it’s still deeply negative.

It seems that larger companies like Bloom Energy will have a much easier time outpacing and holding onto market share as opposed to FCEL. Its cash pile is larger and margins are positive as well.

I went into researching this stock thinking the market must have gotten the wrong idea here, but I have to agree and say that FCEL is a sell when combining all of the negatives I’ve pointed out here so far.

data.title: ECB Preview: Driving Home For Christmas
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

When the European Central Bank meets again next week, some will get a first idea of how their family Christmas dinner might look the week after: stimulating – at times controversial – conversations without any escalating disputes. Like gifts under the Christmas tree, the latest round of ECB projections will be awaited with high expectations by all ECB members. Some will be happier than others; some might even ignore them.

Developments since last meeting call for keeping rates on hold

Let’s leave the Christmas parallels aside for a moment. Since the ECB’s October meeting, the incoming data has done very little to justify any rate change on 18 December. While third quarter GDP growth was clearly stronger than the ECB expected, sentiment indicators also point to continued resilience. At the same time, the fundamentals of the eurozone economy have not changed. US tariffs still weigh on exports, investment is being held back by uncertainty, and 2026 growth remains highly dependent on German fiscal stimulus. Inflation has come in slightly higher than previously expected, but the delayed implementation of the EU Emissions Trading System (ETS2) should push expected inflation by some 0.2 percentage points from 2027 to 2028.

As a result, there’s very little reason for the ECB to change its current monetary policy stance, once again confirming it's in a ‘good place’.

New round of projections unlikely to reflect Schnabel's upside risks to growth and inflation

A fresh round of ECB projections will be an important element next week. While ECB board member Isabel Schnabel said this week that, compared with September, the risks to growth and inflation are tilted to the upside, we doubt this will be reflected in ECB projections. To the contrary, it looks very unlikely that the central bank will change the longer-term growth trajectory of 0.3% quarter-on-quarter in 2026 and 2027. Regarding inflation, the 2026 forecast may be raised slightly (from 1.7% year-on-year in the September projections), while 2027 should be revised downward due to the delayed implementation of ETS2. However, with inflation forecasts for 2026 and 2027 clearly below 2%, it’s difficult to quantify upside risks.

In her Bloomberg interview this week, Schnabel unexpectedly gave the upcoming ECB debate a hawkish tilt. She said she agreed with market expectations that the next move would be a hike, without providing any indication of timing. This comment, however, will be hard to find in the new ECB projections. It also doesn’t echo the sentiment of the minutes of the October meeting, which clearly had a dovish, not a hawkish, bias. This suggests that views at the ECB are diverging, not converging, and that remaining on hold appears to be the best compromise. As a result, we expect ECB President Christine Lagarde to refrain from providing any forward guidance or risk assessment of the inflation outlook at next week’s meeting.

Our base case continues seeing the ECB on hold for foreseeable future

Looking beyond next week, we don’t think that Schnabel’s comments currently reflect the ECB’s majority view. With expected sub-2% inflation forecasts for the next three years, we see any following ECB rate change as a cut, not a hike, at least through late spring next year. After that, the window for rate cuts will likely close, and fiscal stimulus that meets supply side constraints could bring back inflationary pressures. But that is a 2027 story, rather than one for 2026.

Musical chairs is on again

Finally, something that’s probably (for now) more for the ‘rumours and gossips’ corner: Schnabel’s comment that she would stand ready as a potential successor to Lagarde has sparked the musical chairs debate. Many seem to have overlooked the European Treaties, which clearly state that Executive Board members, including the ECB president and vice-president, are appointed for a single, non-renewable term.

More than 20 years ago, a debate arose over whether then-vice president Christian Noyer could become ECB president instead of the designated Jean-Claude Trichet. Legal opinions differed, but a key difference between then and now is that Noyer did not serve a full eight-year term at the ECB. This is because Executive Board members were phased in when the ECB was established. At the current juncture, trying to bend any Treaty rules by arguing that there’s a difference between an ‘ordinary’ Executive Board member and the presidency, just to allow an Executive Board member to become president, looks highly unlikely. Not everything legally possible is politically desirable.

Just like your normal Christmas dinner

All in all, next week’s ECB meeting promises to have all the ingredients of a typical family gathering at Christmas: stimulating and at times controversial conversations, a few rumours and a bit of gossip, accompanied by vague talks about the future but no hard action.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Mama's Creations Is Making Credible Progress In Achieving Its $1 Billion Dream
data.description:
So, how do you feel about a company that aspires to greatness?

If it’s an AI company, you might get excited. Or you might worry if a bubble is about to burst.

Suppose it’s not necessarily AI. Maybe it’s AI adjacent or general tech.

You might smile at the prospect of a break from AI fatigue. Or you might wonder if the company is just dreaming.

Will regular people ever buy a VisionPro spatial computer from Apple Inc. (AAPL)? Will the Metaverse that inspired Facebook to change its name to Meta Platforms, Inc. (META) ever become a serious thing? META is paring back its recently huge bets on that.

Suppose the growth aspirant isn’t even a tech company? Food, perhaps.

Seriously?

Maybe if the chef could be a Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) Optimus robot and the meal could be delivered by one of Alphabet Inc.'s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Waymo taxis — Imagine those two companies teaming up!

But how about your basic grocery store deli counter? Is there any possible way you’d believe a company in that business could grow from a mere $151.3 million of revenue in the trailing 12 months to $1 billion by 2030?

Mama's Creations, Inc. (MAMA) is pushing that sort of vision.

I couldn’t blame you if you rolled your eyes at that. We don’t expect that sort of growth to happen for companies like MAMA.

But then, after the market closed on 12/8/25, MAMA delivered…

Blowout Results and a Hefty Dose of Credibility

I wasn’t shocked. I said “Buy” for MAMA on 9/10/25. And I’ve consistently rated the stock that way since I first covered it on 6/20/24.

I have to admit it was a nervous “Buy.” I openly worried that MAMA was setting its very-high-P/E stock up for a hammering if the company proved unable to deliver on all the margin improvement it was forecasting.

You’d think a high P/E (62.13 when I published on 8/10/25) meant investors were anticipating great things. But…

Judging from the stock’s reaction on 12/9/25 — up 28% — the strength seems to have taken the market by surprise.

(For the record, the stock has been making by previous “Buy” ratings look good. The stock is up about 92% since my 6/20/24 write-up (versus 26% for the S&P 500). And it's up 40%, versus a 5% S&P 500 gain, since 9/10/25.)

Not surprisingly, the stock gave up some of its 12/9/25 gain the next day. (It dropped about 9% on 12/10.) One has to assume that many existing shareholders jumped on a wonderful opportunity to take profits. But let’s go back to the bigger picture…

Apparently, many had been treating MAMA’s oft-repeated visions as low-tech versions of Metaverse/VisionPro-like wishful thinking.

Then came the latest quarter.

The basic numbers, Revenue year-to-year growth of 50% (about 20% ex a big acquisition — see below) is consistent with the kind of growth rates MAMA should post if it’s to achieve its $1 billion aspiration.

But that’s the least of the good news…

The details, the reasons why the numbers came in so strongly, are the main things here. They show MAMA is really doing what it must if it is to realize its long-term vision.

Putting MAMA’s Vision in Context

MAMA’s 4/25 Investor Day presentation slides set the stage for the anticipated growth.

It starts with the food products MAMA offers.

There’s nothing exotic here. You’ve probably seen these, or similar offerings, at your local supermarket’s deli counter.

Like any company in this kind of business, one would expect the assortment of offerings to evolve and expand. Newness is a fact of life.

And, of course, any company like this needs facilities to produce what it sells.

That’s all well and good. But nothing to this point is likely to have rocked your world. At least it hasn’t been like what probably happened the first time you saw serious generative AI.

But here’s the thing….

I don’t know how much you, or people you know, buy at supermarket deli counters. However, whatever your answer, MAMA believes you will buy a lot more in the future…

Secular Demand Growth

In my 9/10/25 write-up, as well as in prior reports, I discussed increasing demand for fresh deli counter offerings.

To briefly summarize, industry data shows…

  • Private brands have been outgrowing national brands,
  • Refrigerated products, a key MAMA category, have been especially strong,
  • Most adults associate refrigerated products with high quality and health benefits,
  • Protein, a key MAMA offering, is the highest-priority, most sought-after nutrient, and…
  • Home dining has been gaining ground at the expense of more expensive restaurant meals.

Regarding the last bullet point, CEO Adam Michaels, on the 12/8/25 earnings call, said:

Even major restaurant operators like Chipotle noted on their most recent earnings calls that they're not losing guests to other chains. They're losing trips to grocery and food at-home occasions. That dynamic directly benefits us.

Industry data shows that the share of shoppers replacing a restaurant meal with deli prepared foods has more than doubled since 2017. Consumers want speed, value, freshness and the ability to shop for the rest of the household at the same time. Fully cooked meats grew 4.8% over the past year. Chicken remains the top performer in the category, and the overall retail foodservice segment has grown to over $52 billion. These are the exact spaces where Mama's competes and wins.

(I checked the latest Chipotle Mexican Grill, Inc. (CMG) earnings call. Michaels' remarks about CMG are accurate.)

Capturing a Bigger Piece of That Pie

Here are things MAMA has done lately (as discussed on the 12/8/25 earnings call) that push the company in the right direction and add credibility to its long-term $1 billion revenue goal.

(1) Scaling Up Its Production Capabilities

Obviously, MAMA can’t sell what it can’t produce.

MAMA had been upgrading two existing facilities.

Then, on 9/2/25, MAMA dramatically upsized by acquiring Crown I Enterprises. Crown, like MAMA, produces food sold at deli counters.

But beyond that, it brings to MAMA a 42,000-square-foot facility in Bay Shore, New York.

Operations were quickly integrated, so much so that the company now speaks of its “1 plant 3 location strategy.”

It’s been…

transitioning some East Rutherford and some Farmingdale production to Bay Shore, unlocking capacity, reducing overtime and increasing absorption across our network…In 3 short months, you can no longer see where one plant begins and the other ends. We are one plant, delivering on our shared one-stop shop strategy. The team is already working through even more synergy capture opportunities, and we expect to lift Bay Shore's gross margin towards our historical corporate range over the next year.

Michales added, on the call, that MAMA successfully transitioned…

from a make-to-order to a make-to-stock organization. This means … we have now created inventory stock of our highest velocity items, resulting in higher service levels for our customers and lower overtime for our operations because we're anticipating our customers' needs.

As noted above, in my last report, I fretted quite a bit about MAMA’s margin-enhancement goals.

Managing commodity input prices helps. On the 12/8/25 earnings call, Michaels said MAMA is “in final negotiations with our commodity suppliers to lock in agreements for calendar '26…” That would add stability to costs.

Even so, many companies encounter snags as they pursue programs like this. That’s still a risk factor here, but for what it’s worth, the effort looks to be off to a good start.

(2) Broadening Distribution

A big – make that huge – aspect of MAMA's growth strategy involves broadening distribution. The company will need to get into more and bigger grocery stores.

That’s already happening. Some progress is based on MAMA’s own initiatives. Others reflect the benefit of having Crown I on board. The latter has long sold to many premium channels that MAMA had not yet penetrated.

Regarding penetrating bigger groceries, Michaels said…

In Q3, we exceeded our goal of adding not 1 but 2 Tier 1 national retailers. The first is Target, where we have confirmed 2 branded sleeve items to begin shipping in February with a staged rollout to 1,995 stores and additional items in the final setup stages for later distribution.

In Food Lion, another major national retailer, we're entering 1,100 stores this month with 2 new branded chicken items as well as rolling out 3 branded sleeves starting at 400 stores.

* * * *

The club channel was another bright spot. [A]fter a successful 5-region rotation of our branded cheese stuffed chicken meatballs in Q3, our first national Costco MVM with branded beef meatballs hit in Q4 and is already creating a noticeable lift in trial and brand awareness…. [O] ur production is ahead of schedule, creating confidence that we can deliver whatever Costco needs whenever and wherever they need it. Costco continues to be one of our strongest strategic partners, and the MVM confirms their confidence in our ability to execute at a national scale.

(Note: MVM refers to Multi Vendor Mailer. Essentially, this is a coupon book retailers use to promote – boost volume – of particular items.)

These are huge developments. Further progress toward $1 billion is going to depend heavily on more success here. So it’s great to see that MAMA is off and running.

The Numbers

Here are the quarterly Revenue and EPS trends.

I get that the fourth quarter 2025 EPS estimate doesn’t impress. But take it with a grain of salt. MAMA did not provide guidance. And while five analysts are producing quarterly revenue estimates, only one is estimating EPS.

Welcome to the wonderful world of microcaps!

(But MAMA is getting out on the analyst conference circuit. One of the bullet-point highlights in MAMA’s 12/8/25 earnings release discussed this. So, future estimates may benefit from more analyst TLC, tender loving care).

Here are historical and forward growth rate comparisons.

(I compare MAMA to a group of companies I selected from among those in the medians among companies in the Packaged Foods and Meats industry that like MAMA, aim at main courses. I dropped those that emphasized snacks, desserts, spices, etc. I also compare MAMA to the medians of companies in the portfolio of the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF (SPY). Because MAMA is small, I also compare it to the medians of companies in the portfolio of the State Street® SPDR® Portfolio S&P 600™ Small Cap ETF (SPSM).

There are no great surprises in the preceding two tables.

Historical revenues look fine. But historic bottom-line figures are less so. Fixed-cost coverage is the bane of a micro-cap’s existence!

Forward numbers look a lot better.

Here are the core fundamentals.

We see that MAMA has been prudent about the use of debt. These ratios look comparatively good.

MAMA’s Returns on assets and equity are especially impressive. This micro-cap beat the SPY benchmarks — we have to tip our hats to that. And, I expect MAMA’s return metrics to improve in the future as the company continues to scale up (which will mean better fixed cost coverage).

Risk

Here are the financial statement highlights.

This is a testament to prudent financial management. As small as MAMA is, we see that the company has kept its debt and interest coverage reasonable (better than reasonable in the more recent years).

And at the same time, it finished the latest period with a higher cash position, even after having completed the Crown I acquisition. (Fortunately, MAMA was able to finance part of the deal with new stock.)

So, financial risk here is modest given MAMA’s prudent approach to funding its growth.

Execution risk is present, as for all growing companies.

Ditto sentiment risk. For MAMA, expect investors to keep a close watch on margin progress and react badly to any future snags.

I’m going to add acquisition risk to the mix. MAMA has long expressed a willingness to acquire. So, Crown I is not likely to be the company’s last purchase. And every acquisition brings with it a whole new set of execution/integration risks.

What to do About MAMA Stock

Here are MAMA’s valuation ratios.

Ouch!

They were high in the past. They’re higher now.

But there is a consolation.

If for P/E FWD, instead of using EPS for the next four quarters, we skip the 2025 fourth quarter and swap in the fourth quarter estimate for next year (i.e., we divide the price by the estimate for the full fiscal year ending January 2027), the ratio drops to a still high but less frightening 47.7.

In that case, we’d be able to get a 2.00 PEG FWD if the long-term EPS growth rate comes in at about 24%. It’s a lot easier to get comfortable with that considering the progress MAMA is making toward its ambitious growth plans.

Such a small adjustment makes a big difference when we talk about companies experiencing meaningful growth.

(Moral of the story: You can’t always stick rigidly to numbers that come out of a database and get processed through a pre-determined algorithm. Sometimes, you really need to look at the business and figure out if any adjustments are warranted. Part V of the classic Graham & Dodd Security Analysis text was all about making common sense adjustments.)

Here’s the price chart.

The stock’s technical picture improved suddenly and boldly leading into and coming out of the latest quarterly report.

The 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) crossed above the 50-day EMA.

The previously lackluster Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) indicators both blasted off into very bullish territory. (Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.)

I expect all these indicators to settle back. So I won’t recommend to stock based on their current readings.

But I can see more sustainably better technical indications emerging down the road assuming MAMA continues to progress and get its story out via the Wall Street conference circuit.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding, or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I still see MAMA as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market, albeit with some bumps here and there, as some future quarters may not live up to the latest one. But based on the long-term perspective I’ve been and still am maintaining for MAMA…

I translate all this to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating MAMA as a “Buy.”

data.title: Accenture Q1 Earnings Preview: Textbook Value Trap
data.description:
Since my last Accenture (ACN) analysis, the stock has lost -22% in price. I issued a Hold rating at the time due to my recognition of low return prospects. Even though the investment is now structurally undervalued, I see little reason for near-term re-rating and so reissue a Hold rating in the current macro environment that I view as overvalued and due for correction. The following report acts as a Q1 2026 earnings preview.

Outlook

Accenture heads into Q1 FY26 - expected 12/18/2025 with $3.73 in normalized EPS (up 3.9% year-over-year) and $18.5 billion in revenue - on the back of FY25 growth of 7% year-over-year and new contracts hitting near-record levels, but management now guides to only 2-5% growth in the coming year while launching a large restructuring. This has driven a nearly 30% share-price decline as investors reset their expectations for growth and margins, but I don't view this as a value opportunity-I view it as a "value trap."

The stock is trading at about $270/share at 21x TTM earnings and 19.5x forward earnings, with a free cash flow yield of about 6.5%. However, the forward P/E non-GAAP ratio is 33% higher than the sector median, so even while there is relative value on pure earnings, it is the growth that is concerning to me and prevents up-rating and alpha as a result.

That said, given that expectations are already reduced, the pace of new contract signings and the believability of the AI monetization path could positively re-rate the stock. The most obvious potential upside catalyst is the revision of its prior 2-5% revenue growth forecast to reflect higher growth expectations, though this is unlikely. However, with the stock already pricing in low expectations, it's offering positive asymmetry, with the greatest risk being consolidation or "dead money."

Even on consensus, the analysts are pretty clear on this one: Accenture is facing growth moderation, and there is no margin of safety in sentiment as a result, particularly after the recent 12.5% gain since September. In fact, I would use pre-earnings as a chance to exit and redeploy capital if one bought at recent lows, as alpha is certainly limited from here and rests on making "bets" rather than any form of sentiment arbitrage, the latter of which I specialize in.

Surveys of technology leaders point to IT budgets growing faster into 2026 due to AI, but I think sentiment is a little overextended here, and we are going to see moderation leading into 2027, meaning that AI budgets at enterprises could deflate as the macro consensus on AI build-out becomes "moderate long-term growth," not "aggressive boom and cyclical busts." Accenture reports that its AI work now makes up roughly 4% of revenue and about 8% of bookings, up at triple-digit rates year-over-year, but still small against the $70B revenue base.

Beyond the tech environment, the main macro headwind is tighter budgets and contract reviews in the U.S. federal and wider public sector; management has flagged this as a roughly 1-1.5 ppt drag on next year's revenue growth.

Accenture's new contract signings are good, but this is not blockbuster growth, and this is unlikely a high-alpha trade in the near term. The bulls will argue that a book-to-bill ratio around 1.2x combined with large, multi-service deals, underpins a durable multi-year pipeline. The bears will argue that if conversion from bookings to revenue were strong, management would not be guiding to only 2-5% local-currency growth. I am somewhere in the middle, not seeing any significant stock mispricing worth capitalizing on and viewing this as a "good business to own" not an "elite stock to trade."

In the medium term there is an inflection catalyst, but it will be gradual and not necessarily alpha-producing at this entry point given the macro constraints on top-line growth in the current climate. Over the next 12-24 months, margins should be viewed as flat to slightly up rather than entering a new, structurally higher range, while embedding AI into delivery and internal functions will reduce human hours per dollar of revenue over the long term.

Price Target

Using the first two charts, I have smoothed the long-term trend on the price chart to create a $310 12-month price target. The present price is $270, so the implied upside is 15%, and my rating is a Hold as a result.

In the base case, for this quarter, it's good enough to sustain sentiment, but likely won't cause any meaningful stock price movement other than a minor bump or decline based on headline numbers. There's nothing really substantial here to invest in pre-earnings unless you're betting on a narrative/strategy shift that is presently undisclosed (not a winning long-term strategy for most people).

Conclusion: Hold

There's a good business here, but it's not a great investment if you're seeking alpha in the markets and definitely not a good investment if you're seeking elite trading activity. We need a positive re-rating catalyst, which would have to come operationally, and predicting that is a losing trading strategy. As a result, just because we are looking at low valuation metrics relative to the sector, the forward PEG non-GAAP ratio being above the sector is the tell that there is no reliable elite alpha mispricing here based on sentiment (again, that's my specialty and how I derive my high Nasdaq alpha, so if you are looking for more forensic operational analysis, other analysts can give you that).

In closing, I would like to present you with a bit of wisdom. Just like being in the army, you are best specialized as a trader and investor. To be a generalist leaves you unfortunately average. In markets, it is differentiation in meritocratic pursuits that derives the "moat," and it is the moat that derives and sustains alpha in portfolios over time. Another specialist may rate Accenture stock a buy at this juncture, because they know something I don't, but for me, it's a hold.
